serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one sotong head on a stick, approximately 60 g, as sold by Old Chang Kee and similar Singaporean snack stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, fried — nutrient composition; adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150) and HPB Singapore Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food (Squid, fried)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sotong Head On Stick is a battered and deep-fried squid head product sold by Old Chang Kee. No exact entry exists in HPB Foodo or USDA for this specific product. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo 'Squid, fried' and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fried squid data (NDB 15150). Batter coating increases carbohydrate and energy relative to plain fried squid. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for a battered fried squid head snack.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of battered fried seafood — analogous estimate based on battered fried fish and squid products; University of Sydney GI Database reference for battered seafood
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sotong head on stick. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous battered fried seafood products in the University of Sydney GI Database. Battered fried fish and squid products typically exhibit low GI (35–45) due to high protein content moderating glucose response and the relatively low net carbohydrate load of the batter. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 6.6) / 100 ≈ 2.6, where carbohydrate per 60 g serving = 11.0 × 0.60 = 6.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~228 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of sotong head on stick due to its moderate sodium content from seasoning and batter; one piece per occasion is advisable within a low-sodium dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content from batter
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sotong head on stick has a low estimated glycemic index and a glycemic load of approximately 2.6 per serving, making it a relatively safe occasional snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in single-piece portions without additional high-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~195 mg per 100 g) and deep-frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in dietary cholesterol and the deep-frying process adds saturated and trans fats; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and prefer grilled or steamed squid preparations where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sotong Head On Stick (also known as Sotong Head OnStik) is a popular Singaporean street snack consisting of a whole squid head coated in a seasoned batter and deep-fried, then served on a bamboo skewer. It is most closely associated with Old Chang Kee, a well-known Singaporean snack chain, and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and convenience outlets across Singapore. The squid head provides a chewy, tender texture with a slightly briny seafood flavour, while the batter adds a crispy, golden exterior. The snack is typically eaten hot and is a common on-the-go food item. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy snack (~175 kcal per 100 g) with a good protein content (~14.5 g per 100 g) from the squid, moderate fat from deep-frying (~8.2 g per 100 g), and a relatively low carbohydrate load (~11 g per 100 g) from the batter. Sodium is moderate at ~380 mg per 100 g, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (~40), making it a relatively diabetes-friendly snack in single-piece portions. Squid is a natural source of vitamin B12, phosphorus, and zinc. The dish is halal-certified when sold by Old Chang Kee and is dairy-free, though it contains gluten from the batter and molluscs as a major allergen. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.
common_user_queries: What is sotong head on stick?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sotong Head On Stick exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo 'Squid, fried' and USDA SR Legacy fried squid data, adjusted for batter coating. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for battered fried seafood. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is well-grounded in two approved primary sources and the food composition is straightforward.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo (Squid, fried) and USDA SR Legacy NDB 15150 (Squid, fried), adjusted upward for carbohydrate and energy to account for batter coating typical of Old Chang Kee-style products. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not consistently reported for fried squid in the source databases. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo squid entry. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food category. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases. 8. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero — squid contains negligible vitamin C and this is consistent with both HPB and USDA source data. 9. GI is an analogous estimate (40, low) based on battered fried seafood in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this product. 10. Halal status reflects Old Chang Kee's halal certification; other vendors may differ. 11. Cholesterol content (~195 mg/100 g) is characteristic of squid and is relevant for cardiovascular health guidance.
